topics.title: Integers & Place Value
topics.description: Understand place value, ordering integers, and applying BIDMAS or operation hierarchies.

foundation.title: Place Value Columns
foundation.content: Each position in a number represents a power of 10. Columns include Thousands, Hundreds, Tens, Units.
foundation.tip: Use a place value grid if you find decimals or large numbers tricky.

foundation.title: BIDMAS / Order of Operations
foundation.content: Always perform calculations in this order: Brackets, Indices, Division/Multiplication (left to right), Addition/Subtraction (left to right).
foundation.tip: Multiplication does NOT take priority over division; work left to right for those.

higher.title: Complex Order of Operations
higher.content: Apply index laws, algebraic groupings, and fractional boundaries within multi-step operation order problems.
higher.tip: Treat the top and bottom of a large fraction as if they are inside hidden brackets.

hacks.title: Left-to-Right Tiebreaker
hacks.content: When only addition/subtraction or multiplication/division remain, work strictly from left to right to prevent errors.

advanced.title: Mathematical Proof Foundations
advanced.content: Use integer properties to demonstrate general arithmetic truths algebraically.

topics.title: Primes, HCF & LCM
topics.description: Find prime factors, Highest Common Factors (HCF), and Lowest Common Multiples (LCM).

foundation.title: Prime Factor Decomposition
foundation.content: Break any number down into a product of prime numbers using a factor tree.
foundation.tip: Circle the prime numbers at the end of each branch so you do not lose them.

foundation.title: Listing for HCF and LCM
foundation.content: Find HCF by listing all factors and picking the highest. Find LCM by listing multiples and picking the lowest matching value.
foundation.tip: Listing works best for smaller numbers; use Venn diagrams for larger ones.

higher.title: Venn Diagrams for HCF & LCM
higher.content: Put common prime factors in the intersection of a Venn Diagram. HCF is the product of the center numbers. LCM is the product of ALL numbers in the circles.
higher.tip: Ensure numbers that appear multiple times are represented accurately in power sets.

hacks.title: The Product Formula
hacks.content: For any two numbers A and B: A × B = HCF(A,B) × LCM(A,B). If you know three values, you can find the fourth instantly.

advanced.title: Cryptographic Primes
advanced.content: Understand how prime factor limits underpin modern digital security and RSA encryption keys.

topics.title: Fractions, Decimals & Percentages
topics.description: Convert, compare, and perform four-operation arithmetic across fractions, decimals, and percentages.

foundation.title: Adding & Subtracting Fractions
foundation.content: Find a common denominator first, adjust the numerators, combine them, and simplify.
foundation.tip: Multiplying the two denominators always gives a common denominator, though not always the lowest.

foundation.title: Multiplying & Dividing Fractions
foundation.content: Multiply straight across for multiplication. For division, flip the second fraction and multiply (Keep-Change-Flip).
foundation.tip: Simplify your fractions before multiplying to keep numbers smaller and easier to handle.

higher.title: Recurring Decimals to Fractions
higher.content: Set x equal to the decimal. Multiply by a power of 10 to shift one full repeating loop, subtract x to clear the recurrence, and solve.
higher.tip: The number of repeating digits tells you whether to multiply by 10, 100, or 1000.

hacks.title: The Cross-Multiplication Comparison
hacks.content: To see if a/b or c/d is larger, calculate a×d and b×c. Whichever product is bigger points to the larger fraction.

advanced.title: Infinite Geometric Progressions
advanced.content: See how recurring decimals are actually sums of infinite geometric series where common ratios are fractions of 10.

topics.title: Indices & Standard Form
topics.description: Master index laws, zero, negative, fractional indices, and reading/writing values in standard form.

foundation.title: Basic Laws of Indices
foundation.content: When multiplying, add powers. When dividing, subtract powers. When raising a power to a power, multiply them.
foundation.formula: x^a × x^b = x^(a+b), x^a ÷ x^b = x^(a-b), (x^a)^b = x^(ab)
foundation.tip: These rules only work when the base numbers or variables are identical.

foundation.title: Converting Standard Form
foundation.content: Write numbers as a value between 1 and 10 multiplied by a power of 10.
foundation.tip: A positive index means a large number; a negative index means a tiny number closer to zero.

higher.title: Negative & Fractional Indices
higher.content: A negative index means the reciprocal. A fractional index means a root, where the denominator is the root and the numerator is the power.
higher.tip: Deal with the root part first to keep numbers smaller before applying the power.

hacks.title: Power of Zero Rule
hacks.content: Anything to the power of 0 is always 1. x⁰ = 1, 5⁰ = 1, (123x)⁰ = 1. Do not let big expressions trick you!

advanced.title: Exponential Equations
advanced.content: Solve equations where the unknown variable is an index by converting bases to identical prime foundations.

topics.title: Surds & Irrational Numbers
topics.description: Simplify surds, expand expressions containing surds, and rationalise denominators.

foundation.title: Introduction to Square Roots
foundation.content: A square root of a number multiplied by itself gives the original number. Perfect squares have integer roots.
foundation.tip: Know your square numbers up to 15!

higher.title: Simplifying Surds
higher.content: Find the largest perfect square factor of the number under the root and take it outside.
higher.tip: Always look for 4, 9, 16, 25, or 36 as factors.

higher.title: Rationalising Denominators
higher.content: Multiply the top and bottom of the fraction by the surd on the bottom to remove the root from the denominator.
higher.tip: For denominators like a + √b, multiply by its conjugate a - √b.

hacks.title: Conjugate Pair Trick
hacks.content: Multiplying (a + √b)(a - √b) always results in an integer: a² - b. Eliminates the middle surd term completely.

advanced.title: Algebraic Surds
advanced.content: Simplify expressions where variables are under roots using identical laws of indices and surds.

topics.title: Bounds & Error Intervals
topics.description: Calculate upper and lower bounds for rounded values and express structural error intervals.

foundation.title: Finding Single Bounds
foundation.content: To find upper and lower bounds, find half of the unit the value was rounded to, then add it for the upper bound and subtract it for the lower bound.
foundation.example: Mass = 70kg to the nearest 10kg. Half unit = 5kg. LB = 65kg, UB = 75kg.
foundation.tip: The upper bound is the limit the value cannot quite reach, written using inequalities like x < 75.

higher.title: Calculations with Bounds
higher.content: For maximum combined values: add UB+UB, multiply UB×UB, or divide UB÷LB. For minimum combined values: subtract LB-UB, or divide LB÷UB.
higher.example: Speed = Distance ÷ Time. Max Speed = Max Distance ÷ Min Time.
higher.tip: Always substitute the individual bounds into your formula before doing the arithmetic.

hacks.title: Division Bound Rule
hacks.content: To get the biggest value from a division, use the biggest numerator possible divided by the smallest denominator possible.

advanced.title: Truncation vs Rounding Error Intervals
advanced.content: Contrast inequalities generated by standard decimal rounding rules against those built from absolute floor truncation systems.

topics.title: Expanding & Simplifying
topics.description: Collect like terms, multiply single or double brackets, and simplify algebraic terms.

foundation.title: Collecting Like Terms
foundation.content: You can only add or subtract terms with identical variables and powers.
foundation.tip: Circle or underline like terms along with the operational sign right in front of them.

foundation.title: Single Bracket Expansion
foundation.content: Multiply the term on the outside of the bracket by every single term inside the bracket.
foundation.tip: Be extremely careful when multiplying by a negative term outside brackets.

higher.title: Triple Bracket Expansion
higher.content: Expand two brackets first, simplify that result completely, and then multiply that new trinomial expression by the third remaining bracket.
higher.tip: Keep your work clean and organized in rows to avoid missing cross-product terms.

hacks.title: FOIL Method for Double Brackets
hacks.content: Remember First, Outer, Inner, Last when expanding two brackets to ensure you check all four multiplication steps.

advanced.title: Binomial Theorem Basics
advanced.content: Spot simple expansion coefficient patterns using combinations from Pascal's Triangle for higher powers.

topics.title: Factorising Expressions
topics.description: Extract common factors, factorise simple and complex quadratics, and recognize the difference of two squares.

foundation.title: Common Factor Extraction
foundation.content: Find the Highest Common Factor of numbers and letters, and pull it outside a single set of brackets.
foundation.tip: Check your answer by expanding it back out; it should match the original question.

foundation.title: Basic Quadratic Factorising
foundation.content: For quadratics like x² + bx + c, find two numbers that multiply to c and add to b to place inside two sets of brackets.
foundation.tip: If c is negative, your two bracket numbers must have opposite signs.

higher.title: Complex Quadratic Factorising
higher.content: When a > 1 in ax² + bx + c, split the middle term bx using factors of (a × c) to complete factorisation by grouping.
higher.tip: Always verify if there is an overall common numerical factor you can pull out first to simplify things.

hacks.title: Difference of Two Squares (DOTS)
hacks.content: Whenever you see two perfect squares subtracted with no middle term, it factorises instantly into matching brackets with opposite signs: x² - a² = (x-a)(x+a).

advanced.title: Factorising Higher Degree Polynomials
advanced.content: Apply algebraic remainder concepts to isolate matching terms out of advanced multi-variable expressions.

topics.title: Linear Equations
topics.description: Solve linear equations with unknowns on one or both sides, including fractional configurations.

foundation.title: Unknowns on One Side
foundation.content: Isolate the variable by doing inverse operations to both sides of the equation.
foundation.tip: Always work backward against standard arithmetic operation priority.

foundation.title: Unknowns on Both Sides
foundation.content: Move all terms with variables to one side and numbers to the other, ideally moving the smaller coefficient.
foundation.tip: Subtracting the smaller variable term keeps your coefficients positive, avoiding minus sign slips.

higher.title: Complex Fractional Linear Equations
higher.content: Clear denominators completely by multiplying every term by the Lowest Common Multiple of all the denominators.
higher.tip: Be sure to multiply single stand-alone constants by the LCM as well.

hacks.title: The Equal Balance Rule
hacks.content: Treat the equals sign like a balance scale. Whatever operation you apply to one side, you must apply to the other side to keep it true.

advanced.title: Matrix Representations
advanced.content: Explore how multi-variable linear arrays map to transformation matrices used widely across computer physics software.

topics.title: Rearranging Formulae
topics.description: Change the subject of a formula, including cases where the target variable appears multiple times.

foundation.title: Simple Two-Step Rearranging
foundation.content: Isolate the target variable by performing matching inverse operations on both sides.
foundation.example: Make x the subject of y = 3x - 5. Add 5 -> y + 5 = 3x. Divide by 3 -> x = (y + 5)/3.
foundation.tip: Undo additions or subtractions before dealing with coefficients.

higher.title: Subject Appearing Twice
higher.content: Gather all terms containing the new subject on one side, move everything else to the opposite side, and factorise out the subject.
higher.example: Make x the subject of ax + b = cx + d -> ax - cx = d - b -> x(a - c) = d - b -> x = (d - b)/(a - c)
higher.tip: Factorising is the key step to turning two appearances of a variable into a single one.

hacks.title: Factorisation Pivot Trick
hacks.content: If the variable you want to make the subject is stuck inside multiple terms, group them together and factorise. This isolates it instantly.

advanced.title: Inverting Composed Operator Sets
advanced.content: Formulate algebraic inverse rules across composite non-linear functions by carefully reversing operational paths.

topics.title: Quadratic Equations
topics.description: Solve quadratics by factorising, completing the square, and using the formula.

foundation.title: What is a Quadratic?
foundation.content: A quadratic equation contains an x² term as its highest power. Standard form is ax² + bx + c = 0.
foundation.tip: The coefficient 'a' cannot be zero, or it becomes a linear equation.

foundation.title: Solving by Factorising
foundation.content: Factorise the quadratic into two brackets, set it to zero, and find the values that make each bracket zero.
foundation.tip: Always make sure the equation equals zero before you start factorising.

higher.title: The Quadratic Formula
higher.content: Use this formula to find solutions for any quadratic equation, especially when it will not factorise cleanly.
higher.tip: Remember to use brackets around negative numbers under the square root in your calculator.

higher.title: Completing the Square
higher.content: Rewrite a quadratic in vertex form by taking half of the middle coefficient.
higher.tip: This method is great for finding the coordinates of a graph's turning point.

hacks.title: Discriminant Check Shortcut
hacks.content: Look at b² - 4ac. If it is greater than 0, you get two real answers; if it equals 0, you get one answer; if it is less than 0, there are no real solutions.

advanced.title: Hidden Quadratics
advanced.content: Solve complex equations like x⁴ - 5x² + 4 = 0 by substituting u = x² to make it a quadratic equation first.

topics.title: Simultaneous Equations
topics.description: Solve pairs of simultaneous linear or non-linear equations using elimination or substitution.

foundation.title: Elimination Method
foundation.content: Multiply one or both equations to make the coefficients of one variable match, then add or subtract the equations to eliminate that variable.
foundation.example: Eq1: 2x+y=7, Eq2: 3x-y=3. Add them -> 5x=10 -> x=2. Substitute x back in -> 4+y=7 -> y=3.
foundation.tip: If the signs of the matching coefficients are the Same, Subtract. If they are Opposite, Add (SSS: Same Signs Subtract).

higher.title: Substitution with Linear and Non-Linear
higher.content: Rearrange the linear equation to isolate one variable, then substitute that expression into the quadratic equation to solve it.
higher.example: y = x + 1 and x² + y² = 5 -> x² + (x+1)² = 5 -> 2x² + 2x - 4 = 0 -> x = 1 or x = -2. Find corresponding y values.
higher.tip: Quadratic simultaneous equations usually give you two pairs of matching x and y answers.

hacks.title: The Bracket Safeguard
hacks.content: When substituting a linear expression into a quadratic equation, always place brackets around it before squaring to avoid missing middle expansion terms.

advanced.title: Intersection Interpretations
advanced.content: Understand how the solutions connect to real geometry, like a straight line intersecting or touching a circle.

topics.title: Inequalities
topics.description: Solve linear inequalities, represent solutions on graphs/number lines, and solve quadratic inequalities.

foundation.title: Linear Inequalities
foundation.content: Solve linear inequalities just like normal equations, but remember to flip the inequality sign if you multiply or divide by a negative number.
foundation.tip: Use open circles for < or > on a number line, and solid circles for ≤ or ≥.

foundation.title: Shading Regions on Graphs
foundation.content: Draw boundary lines, then choose a test point like (0,0) to see which region matches the inequality before shading it.
foundation.tip: Use a dashed line for strict inequalities (<, >) and a solid line for inclusive ones (≤, ≥).

higher.title: Quadratic Inequalities
higher.content: Find the critical values by setting the inequality to an equation and solving it. Sketch the curve to see where it goes above or below the x-axis.
higher.example: x² - 5x + 6 > 0 -> roots are 2 and 3. Parabola opens upward, so x < 2 or x > 3.
higher.tip: Do not try to solve it like a linear inequality; always find the roots first.

hacks.title: The Negative Flip Alert
hacks.content: Whenever you multiply or divide both sides of an inequality by a negative number, turn the inequality sign around immediately.

advanced.title: Linear Programming Optimization
advanced.content: Find maximum or minimum values over regional boundaries defined by a set of multiple linear inequalities.

topics.title: Numerical Methods & Iteration
topics.description: Find approximate solutions to equations using iterative processes and recurrence relations.

higher.title: Understanding Recurrence Relations
higher.content: An iteration formula uses the current value x_n to find the next value x_{n+1}.
higher.example: Given x_0 = 1 and x_{n+1} = √(x_n + 2), calculate x_1.
higher.tip: Use the ANS key on your calculator to speed up inputs!

higher.title: Locating Roots
higher.content: If a continuous function changes sign between two values of x, a root lies between those values.
higher.formula: f(a) > 0 and f(b) < 0 -> root between a and b
higher.example: f(x) = x³ - x - 1. f(1) = -1, f(2) = 5. Change of sign proves root exists.
higher.tip: Always explicitly state 'change of sign implies a root' in exams.

hacks.title: Calculator ANS Key
hacks.content: Type the initial value x_0 and press equals. Then type the formula using the 'Ans' button instead of x. Keep pressing '=' to generate x_1, x_2, x_3 instantly.

advanced.title: Cobweb and Staircase Diagrams
advanced.content: Visualise convergence and divergence of iterations by drawing lines between y = x and the curve y = f(x).

topics.title: Angle Properties & Parallel Lines
topics.description: Apply core angle facts, parallel lines rules, and find interior/exterior angles of polygons.

foundation.title: Angles on Lines & Around Points
foundation.content: Angles on a straight line add up to 180°. Angles around a point add up to 360°. Vertically opposite angles are equal.
foundation.example: If two straight lines cross and one angle is 50°, the opposite angle is also 50°.
foundation.tip: Always double check your subtraction from 180 or 360.

foundation.title: Parallel Lines (F, Z, C Shapes)
foundation.content: Alternate angles are equal (Z shape). Corresponding angles are equal (F shape). Allied/Co-interior angles add up to 180° (C shape).
foundation.tip: Make sure you use the official exam terms like 'alternate' or 'corresponding' rather than letters like Z or F.

foundation.title: Angles in Polygons
foundation.content: Exterior angles of any polygon always add up to 360°. The sum of interior angles is found using a formula based on the number of sides.
foundation.tip: For regular polygons, you can find a single interior angle easily by doing 180° minus the exterior angle.

higher.title: Geometric Proof with Angles
higher.content: Construct full algebraic proofs for polygon or line properties using parallel line theorems and variable forms.
higher.tip: Link each step clearly to an official geometric reason.

hacks.title: The Exterior Shortcut
hacks.content: To find the number of sides of a regular polygon quickly, just divide 360 by the exterior angle: n = 360 / Exterior.

advanced.title: Non-Euclidean Geometries
advanced.content: See how angle sum rules change when drawing shapes on curved surfaces instead of flat planes.

topics.title: Perimeter, Area & Volume
topics.description: Calculate area and perimeter of 2D shapes, along with surface area and volume of prisms, cylinders, pyramids, and spheres.

foundation.title: Area of Core 2D Shapes
foundation.content: Know the formulas for triangles, parallelograms, and trapeziums.
foundation.example: Trapezium with a=4cm, b=6cm, h=3cm: Area = 0.5 × (4+6) × 3 = 15 cm²
foundation.tip: Always look out for perpendicular heights; do not use slanted side lengths by mistake.

foundation.title: Volume of Prisms
foundation.content: The volume of any prism is the area of its cross-section multiplied by its length.
foundation.example: A triangular prism with a cross-section area of 10 cm² and length 5 cm has a volume of 50 cm³.
foundation.tip: Remember that volume is measured in cubic units, like cm³ or m³.

higher.title: Pyramids, Cones & Spheres
higher.content: Apply the fractional volume formulas provided on the exam formulas sheet for advanced curved solids.
higher.example: Find the volume of a cone with radius 3cm and height 4cm: Vol = (1/3) × π × 3² × 4 = 12π cm³
higher.tip: Leave your answers in terms of π if the question asks for an exact value.

hacks.title: The Net Breakdown Strategy
hacks.content: To find the surface area of any 3D object safely, sketch out its flat net first, calculate the individual area of each face, and add them up.

advanced.title: Frustum Calculations
advanced.content: Find the volume of a truncated cone or pyramid by subtracting the volume of the smaller missing top cone from the original large cone shape.

topics.title: Right-Angled Triangles
topics.description: Master Pythagoras' Theorem and standard SOHCAHTOA trigonometry to find missing sides and angles.

foundation.title: Pythagoras' Theorem
foundation.content: In any right-angled triangle, the square of the hypotenuse is equal to the sum of the squares of the other two sides.
foundation.tip: The hypotenuse 'c' is always the longest side, directly opposite the 90-degree angle.

foundation.title: SOHCAHTOA Trigonometry Sides
foundation.content: Label sides as Hypotenuse, Opposite, and Adjacent relative to a given angle, and use sine, cosine, or tangent ratios to find missing lengths.
foundation.example: Find Opposite if Hypotenuse=10m and angle=30°. O = 10 × sin(30°) = 5m.
foundation.tip: Set your scientific calculator to Degree mode ('DEG') before doing trig questions.

higher.title: Exact Trigonometric Values
higher.content: Memorise the exact non-decimal values for sine, cosine, and tangent for key angles like 0°, 30°, 45°, 60°, and 90°.
higher.tip: You can use a simple hand trick or sketch quick triangles to remember these without a calculator.

hacks.title: The SOHCAHTOA Triangle Triangles
hacks.content: Cover the letter you want to find in formula triangles (like O over S×H) to quickly see whether you need to multiply or divide.

advanced.title: 3D Pythagoras & Trigonometry
advanced.content: Find diagonals across 3D boxes by applying Pythagoras twice, or calculate angles between lines and planes inside pyramids.

topics.title: Non-Right Triangles
topics.description: Apply the Sine Rule, Cosine Rule, and the trigonometric area formula to find dimensions of non-right-angled triangles.

higher.title: The Sine Rule
higher.content: Use this rule when you have matching opposite pairs of sides and angles.
higher.example: Find side a when A=40°, b=10cm, B=60°. a = (10 × sin(40°)) ÷ sin(60°) = 7.42cm.
higher.tip: Flip the formula upside down to sin(A)/a if you need to find an unknown angle instead of a side.

higher.title: The Cosine Rule
higher.content: Use this rule when you know two sides and the angle between them (SAS), or when you know all three sides (SSS).
higher.tip: Remember to execute the multiplication 2bc·cos(A) completely before subtracting it from the earlier sum.

higher.title: Trigonometric Area Formula
higher.content: Calculate the area of any triangle given two sides and the included angle.
higher.tip: The angle used must be trapped right between the two known sides.

hacks.title: Sine vs Cosine Decision Rule
hacks.content: If you have all three sides or a trapped angle (SAS), use the Cosine Rule. For any other combination, use the Sine Rule.

advanced.title: The Ambiguous Case of the Sine Rule
advanced.content: Be aware that when finding an angle, sin⁻¹ gives an acute angle, but an obtuse option might also exist, found by doing 180° minus that angle.

topics.title: Transformations
topics.description: Perform and describe reflections, rotations, translations, and enlargements.

foundation.title: Translations & Reflections
foundation.content: Translations shift a shape using a column vector. Reflections flip a shape across a specific mirror line like x=2 or y=x.
foundation.example: Translating [3, -1] means moving 3 squares right and 1 square down.
foundation.tip: Count grid lines perpendicularly to the mirror line to keep reflections accurate.

foundation.title: Rotations
foundation.content: Turn a shape around a fixed coordinate point, using a specific angle direction (clockwise or anticlockwise).
foundation.tip: Always use tracing paper in the exam to check your rotations carefully.

higher.title: Negative and Fractional Enlargements
higher.content: Fractional scale factors shrink shapes closer to the center. Negative scale factors project the shape upside down on the opposite side of the center point.
higher.example: Scale factor -2 doubles the size and flips the shape through the center point.
higher.tip: Draw guidelines from the original corners through the center of enlargement to find the new positions.

hacks.title: Full Description Marks Checklist
hacks.content: To get full marks when describing a transformation: Translation needs a vector; Reflection needs a line; Rotation needs an angle, direction, and center; Enlargement needs a scale factor and center.

advanced.title: Invariance Properties
advanced.content: Identify points or lines that do not move at all after a specific sequence of geometric transformations.

topics.title: Similarity & Congruence
topics.description: Prove geometric congruence and calculate lengths, areas, and volumes for mathematically similar shapes.

foundation.title: Congruence Proof Criteria
foundation.content: Two shapes are congruent if they are identical in size and shape. For triangles, prove this using SSS, SAS, ASA, or RHS rules.
foundation.tip: Make sure you state the exact rule abbreviation (like SSS) as your reason to secure full marks.

higher.title: Length, Area & Volume Scale Factors
higher.content: If the linear scale factor is k, the area scale factor is k² and the volume scale factor is k³.
higher.example: If a shape's length is doubled (LSF=2), its area increases by 4 (ASF=4) and its volume increases by 8 (VSF=8).
higher.tip: Find the length scale factor first before trying to solve area or volume adjustments.

hacks.title: Scale Factor Root Trick
hacks.content: If you are given two areas, divide them to find the Area Scale Factor, then take the square root to find the Length Scale Factor.

advanced.title: Formal Geometric Congruence Deductions
advanced.content: Construct multi-stage triangle alignment proofs within circle and tangent structures using formal mathematical notation.

topics.title: Vectors
topics.description: Understand column vectors, add/subtract vectors geometrically, and solve geometric proofs using vectors.

foundation.title: Column Vectors
foundation.content: Vectors describe a movement with direction and size. The top number shows horizontal movement, the bottom shows vertical movement.
foundation.example: [2, -3] means move 2 units right and 3 units down.
foundation.tip: Multiplying a vector by a number scales both parts inside.

higher.title: Vector Geometry Paths
higher.content: To find an unknown vector route, construct an alternative pathway along known vectors from the start point to the endpoint.
higher.example: Vector AB = AO + OB = -a + b
higher.tip: Going against a vector arrow reverses its sign.

hacks.title: Collinear Proof Trick
hacks.content: To prove three points A, B, and C lie on a straight line, show that vector AB is a multiple of vector BC, and state that they share point B.

advanced.title: Ratio Splitting
advanced.content: If a point P splits vector AB in the ratio 2:3, then vector AP = 2/5 of vector AB and PB = 3/5 of vector AB.

topics.title: Circle Theorems
topics.description: Apply geometric properties and formal circle theorems to calculate unknown angles.

foundation.title: Parts of a Circle
foundation.content: Identify radius, diameter, chord, tangent, arc, sector, and segment elements before performing proofs.
foundation.tip: A radius and a tangent meet at exactly 90 degrees.

higher.title: Core Circle Theorems
higher.content:
1. Angle at the centre is twice the angle at the circumference.
2. Angle in a semi-circle is 90 degrees.
3. Angles in the same segment are equal.
higher.tip: Look for isosceles triangles created by drawing multiple radii from the centre.

higher.title: Cyclic Quadrilaterals & Tangents
higher.content:
4. Opposite angles in a cyclic quadrilateral sum to 180 degrees.
5. Alternate Segment Theorem: The angle between a tangent and a chord equals the angle in the alternate segment.
higher.tip: Always write out the exact phrasing of the theorem text as your reason in exams.

hacks.title: The 'Arrowhead' Cue
hacks.content: Whenever you spot an arrowhead shape anchored to the circle perimeter and passing through the centre, instantly apply the 'double angle at centre' rule.

advanced.title: Geometric Proofs
advanced.content: Prove theorems algebraically by introducing variables like x and y alongside base triangle geometry constraints.

topics.title: Mean, Median & Mode
topics.description: Calculate and compare averages from list datasets or grouped frequency tables.

foundation.title: Averages from Lists
foundation.content: Mean is the sum divided by the count. Median is the middle value when ordered. Mode is the most common number. Range is the high minus the low value.
foundation.tip: Always sort your data list first before looking for the median.

foundation.title: Averages from Grouped Tables
foundation.content: Estimate the mean by multiplying each group midpoint by its frequency, adding them up, and dividing by the total frequency.
foundation.tip: Remember, this is an estimate because we do not know the exact raw values inside the groups.

higher.title: Comparing Averages and Spread
higher.content: Use an average (like median) to talk about central trend, and a spread measure (like range or IQR) to discuss consistency.
higher.tip: Always mention context (e.g., test scores, race times) when writing comparison answers.

hacks.title: Total Frequency Divide Warning
hacks.content: When calculating the mean from a table, make sure you divide by the total frequency, NOT by the number of rows in the table.

advanced.title: Skewness and Outliers
advanced.content: Analyse how extreme outlier data points distort the mean while leaving the median stable.

topics.title: Probability Trees & Diagrams
topics.description: Calculate probabilities of single and combined events using sample spaces, Venn diagrams, and tree diagrams.

foundation.title: Basic Probability Scale
foundation.content: Probabilities range from 0 (impossible) to 1 (certain). The probabilities of all mutually exclusive events add up to 1.
foundation.tip: You can write probabilities as fractions, decimals, or percentages, but never as ratios.

foundation.title: Independent Tree Diagrams
foundation.content: Multiply probabilities along the branches to find the chance of combined outcomes.
foundation.tip: Branches coming from the same node must always add up to 1.

higher.title: Dependent Events (No Replacement)
higher.content: Adjust the fractions on the second set of branches because the total number of items has decreased.
higher.example: Pick 2 red counters out of a bag with 4 red and 6 blue: P(Red, Red) = 4/10 × 3/9 = 12/90.
higher.tip: Look out for keywords like 'does not replace' or 'takes another'.

hacks.title: The 'And' / 'Or' Probability Rule
hacks.content: Multiply probabilities when you need one event AND another to happen. Add them when you want one event OR another outcome.

advanced.title: Conditional Probability Proofs
advanced.content: Master formulaic representations for formal conditional systems, written as P(A given B).

topics.title: Cumulative Frequency & Box Plots
topics.description: Construct and interpret cumulative frequency curves and box plots, and estimate the median and interquartile range (IQR).

higher.title: Cumulative Frequency Curves
higher.content: Add up frequencies as you go, plot the points at the UPPER class limits, and join them with a smooth S-shaped curve.
higher.tip: Always read values carefully by drawing lines across from 25%, 50%, and 75% of the total frequency axis.

higher.title: Reading Box Plots
higher.content: A box plot summarizes data using five key values: Minimum, Lower Quartile (LQ), Median, Upper Quartile (UQ), and Maximum.
higher.example: If UQ = 70 and LQ = 40, the Interquartile Range is 30.
higher.tip: The box itself holds the middle 50% of the entire data sample.

hacks.title: The Upper Limit Plot Rule
hacks.content: Never plot cumulative frequency points at the midpoint of a group; always use the upper limit of that group interval.

advanced.title: Identifying Outliers Algebraically
advanced.content: Classify extreme data points as formal outliers if they lie more than 1.5 times the IQR past either quartile line.

topics.title: Histograms
topics.description: Draw and interpret histograms for unequal class intervals using frequency density calculations.

higher.title: Frequency Density Formula
higher.content: Histograms deal with continuous data in uneven groups. The area of each bar shows the frequency, not the height.
higher.example: A group spanning 0-10 has a frequency of 30. Frequency density = 30 / 10 = 3.
higher.tip: Always label your vertical axis as 'Frequency Density'.

higher.title: Estimating the Median
higher.content: Find the cumulative midpoint position, then calculate how far into a specific bar's width you need to go to reach that count.
higher.tip: Assume the data values are spread out evenly across that specific group bar.

hacks.title: Area Equals Frequency Rule
hacks.content: Always remember: Area = Frequency. If a question asks for a count of items, find the combined area of those blocks.

advanced.title: Interpreting Skewness
advanced.content: Analyze asymmetric shapes across histograms to describe real-world sample population patterns.

topics.title: Scatter Graphs & Correlation
topics.description: Plot scatter data, identify correlation types, and draw lines of best fit to estimate missing values.

foundation.title: Types of Correlation
foundation.content: Positive correlation means both values go up together. Negative correlation means as one goes up, the other goes down. No correlation means the points are scattered randomly.
foundation.example: Temperature and ice cream sales show a positive correlation.
foundation.tip: Correlation does not automatically mean that one thing caused the other to happen.

foundation.title: Line of Best Fit
foundation.content: Draw a straight line through the center of the points, keeping roughly the same number of dots above and below your line.
foundation.tip: Always use a ruler, and avoid forcing your line to pass through the origin (0,0) unless it naturally fits the data.

higher.title: Extrapolation Dangers
higher.content: Estimating values outside the range of your original data points is unreliable and should be avoided.
higher.tip: State that estimates outside the data range are unreliable because trends can change.

hacks.title: The Mean Coordination Point
hacks.content: A perfect line of best fit should always pass exactly through the mean average coordinate of x and y from your dataset.

advanced.title: Introduction to Linear Regression
advanced.content: Learn how computer algorithms find the absolute mathematically ideal line of best fit by minimizing squared distances.

topics.title: Ratios & Sharing
topics.description: Simplify ratios, convert ratios to fractions, and share quantities into given ratio parts.

foundation.title: Simplifying Ratios
foundation.content: Divide all parts of a ratio by their highest common factor until they cannot be divided further.
foundation.example: 15 : 20 simplifies to 3 : 4 by dividing both parts by 5.
foundation.tip: Make sure all units are exactly the same before you start simplifying a ratio.

foundation.title: Sharing an Amount
foundation.content: Add up the total parts, divide the total amount by this number to find the value of one part, then multiply to find each share.
foundation.example: Share £60 in the ratio 2:3. Total parts = 5. One part = 60/5 = £12. Shares are £24 and £36.
foundation.tip: Add your final shares back together to make sure they equal the original total amount.

higher.title: Changing Ratios
higher.content: Solve multi-step problems where adding or subtracting quantities shifts the ratio between variables.
higher.example: Initial ratio 1:2. Add 5 to the first part, new ratio becomes 2:3. Set up an equation to find the original values.
higher.tip: Convert ratios into algebraic fractions to solve these changing proportion puzzles easily.

hacks.title: The Three-Step Ratio Plan
hacks.content: Remember: Add parts, Divide total, Multiply shares (ADM: Add, Divide, Multiply) to solve sharing questions quickly.

advanced.title: The Golden Ratio Relationship
advanced.content: Explore the unique properties of the golden ratio φ and its appearances across nature and geometry.

topics.title: Direct & Inverse Proportion
topics.description: Set up and solve algebraic direct and inverse proportion equations using a constant of proportionality k.

foundation.title: Simple Unitary Proportion
foundation.content: Find the value of a single item first to scale quantities up or down accordingly.
foundation.example: If 5 apples cost £2, then 1 apple costs £0.40. Therefore, 8 apples cost 8 × £0.40 = £3.20.
foundation.tip: Keeping things clear with a table can help track units under exam pressure.

higher.title: Algebraic Direct Proportion
higher.content: Write the relationship using the proportionality symbol, convert it to an equation with a constant k, find k using given numbers, and write the final formula.
higher.example: y is directly proportional to x². When x=2, y=12. So 12 = k(2²) -> k=3. Final formula: y = 3x².
higher.tip: Read carefully to see if it is proportional to x, x², or the square root of x.

higher.title: Algebraic Inverse Proportion
higher.content: As one value goes up, the other goes down. Set up your formula with x on the bottom of the fraction.
higher.example: y is inversely proportional to x. When x=3, y=4. So 4 = k/3 -> k=12. Formula: y = 12/x.
higher.tip: In inverse proportion, multiplying the two given x and y values gives you the constant k immediately.

hacks.title: The k-Constant Anchor
hacks.content: Never skip finding k. Treat finding the value of k as your primary goal, and write down the full formula before answering the rest of the question.

advanced.title: Joint Proportion Systems
advanced.content: Handle complex scenarios where a variable depends on multiple other factors at the same time, like y = kxz / w.

topics.title: Capture-Recapture Method
topics.description: Estimate overall population sizes in ecosystems using proportions from tagged sample sets.

foundation.title: The Proportional Principle
foundation.content: Capture a sample, tag them, and release them. Capture a second sample later. The proportion of tagged items in the second sample helps estimate the total population.
foundation.formula: Total Population = (Sample 1 Count × Sample 2 Count) ÷ Tagged Recaptured Count
foundation.example: Catch and tag 20 fish. Next day catch 50 fish, finding 5 tagged. Total = (20 × 50) / 5 = 200 fish.
foundation.tip: Set up two equal fractions to double check your calculations safely.

higher.title: Underlying Assumptions
higher.content: This method assumes the population stays stable (no births, deaths, or migration), tags do not fall off, and tagged animals mix back in thoroughly.
higher.tip: Be ready to list at least two of these assumptions in written exam answers.

hacks.title: Cross-Multiplication Layout
hacks.content: Write it as: Tagged/Total = Recaptured Tagged/Total Recaptured Sample. Cross multiply to isolate your unknown total value immediately.

advanced.title: Bias and Errors
advanced.content: Evaluate how animal behavior (like being trap-shy or attracted to bait) can cause overestimates or underestimates of population sizes.
